inputs.userGoal: Azure IoT Hub edge deployment with AI module for anomaly detection, output events to Cosmos DB, and send SMS alerts via Logic Apps.

architecturePrinciples: Edge-first AI processing to reduce cloud bandwidth and latency
Serverless where possible to minimize operational overhead
Secure by default with managed identity and least-privilege access

resources.name: iot-hub-anomaly-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: S1 Standard tier required for IoT Edge device management, cloud-to-device messaging, device twins, and message routing. Basic tier lacks Edge deployment management and direct methods needed for AI module lifecycle.
alternativesConsidered: F1 Free tier — limited to 8,000 messages/day, no SLA, max 500 device identities
S2 — higher throughput (6M messages/day) but over-provisioned for initial deployment
Azure IoT Central — managed SaaS, less control over Edge AI module deployment
reasoning.tradeoffs: S1 provides 400K messages/day per unit. Can scale by adding units without SKU change. Edge deployment features fully supported.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Root resource — no dependencies on other planned resources.

references.title: Choose the right IoT Hub tier

resources.name: cracranomaly001

reasoning.whyChosen: Container Registry stores the custom AI anomaly detection IoT Edge module image. IoT Edge devices pull module images from ACR. Basic SKU sufficient for dev/small-scale; supports 10 GiB storage.
alternativesConsidered: Standard — 100 GiB storage, higher throughput; needed if many Edge devices pull concurrently
Premium — geo-replication, private endpoints; needed for multi-region or enterprise security
Docker Hub — external dependency, no private network integration
reasoning.tradeoffs: Basic tier limits to 2 webhooks and 10 GiB storage. Sufficient for initial deployment with a single AI module image.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Standalone resource. IoT Hub references it in Edge deployment manifests but no ARM-level dependency.

resources.name: stiotanomalyprod001

reasoning.whyChosen: Required for IoT Hub file uploads and Stream Analytics job checkpointing. StorageV2 is the recommended general-purpose kind. Standard_LRS provides cost-effective local redundancy.
alternativesConsidered: Standard_GRS — geo-redundant; adds cost but useful for DR scenarios
Standard_ZRS — zone-redundant; better availability within a region
Premium_LRS — SSD-backed; unnecessary for log/checkpoint workloads
reasoning.tradeoffs: LRS provides 3 copies within a single data center. Acceptable for non-critical IoT staging data. Upgrade to ZRS/GRS if higher durability needed.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Standalone resource. Referenced by IoT Hub and Stream Analytics.

resources.name: cosmos-anomaly-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Cosmos DB NoSQL API is ideal for storing semi-structured IoT anomaly events with variable schemas. Serverless mode eliminates provisioned throughput cost — pay only per RU consumed, ideal for bursty anomaly event patterns.
alternativesConsidered: Provisioned throughput — predictable cost but requires capacity planning upfront
Azure SQL Database — relational model adds schema rigidity for variable IoT payloads
Azure Table Storage — lower cost but limited querying and no change feed for Logic App triggering
reasoning.tradeoffs: Serverless is single-region only (no geo-replication). 1 GB max per logical partition. Session consistency provides read-your-writes within a session — good balance of consistency and performance for IoT workloads.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Standalone resource. Stream Analytics writes to it; no ARM-level dependency needed.

references.title: Stream Analytics to Cosmos DB output

resources.name: asa-anomaly-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Stream Analytics provides serverless real-time stream processing with native IoT Hub input and Cosmos DB output connectors. Built-in anomaly detection functions (AnomalyDetection_SpikeAndDip) can augment edge AI results. 1 SU sufficient for initial throughput.
alternativesConsidered: Azure Functions — more flexible code but requires custom serialization and connection management
IoT Hub message routing — can route to built-in endpoints but no transformation or anomaly enrichment
Azure Data Explorer — powerful analytics but heavier infrastructure for simple event routing
reasoning.tradeoffs: Stream Analytics charges per streaming unit-hour. 1 SU handles up to 1 MB/s input. Dual output to Cosmos DB (archival) and Service Bus (alerting) enables decoupled downstream processing.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Requires IoT Hub as input source, Cosmos DB as archival output, and Service Bus as alert output.

resources.name: sb-anomaly-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Service Bus queue decouples Stream Analytics anomaly output from Logic App processing. Provides reliable message delivery with at-least-once semantics. Basic tier supports queues which is sufficient for the alert pipeline.
alternativesConsidered: Standard tier — adds topics/subscriptions for fan-out; needed only if multiple consumers
Azure Event Grid — push-based eventing; simpler but less reliable for critical alerts
Storage Queue — cheaper but lacks dead-letter queue and advanced features
reasoning.tradeoffs: Basic tier limits to queues only (no topics). Max message size 256 KB. Sufficient for anomaly alert payloads.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Standalone resource. Referenced by Stream Analytics as output and Logic App as trigger.

resources.name: logic-sms-alert-prod-001

actions: Send SMS via Azure Communication Services connector

reasoning.whyChosen: Consumption Logic App provides serverless workflow with pay-per-execution pricing. Native Service Bus trigger and SMS connectors eliminate custom code. Visual designer enables non-developer maintenance of alert logic.
alternativesConsidered: Standard Logic App — dedicated compute, VNet integration; overkill for simple SMS alerting
Azure Functions — more flexible but requires coding and connector management
Azure Notification Hubs — push notifications, not SMS; different use case
reasoning.tradeoffs: Consumption tier has cold-start latency (~seconds). Workflow definition must be configured post-deployment with SMS connector credentials. Acceptable for alert scenarios where sub-second latency is not required.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Logic App triggers on messages in the Service Bus anomaly-alerts queue.

references.title: Logic Apps Consumption quickstart with Bicep

overallReasoning.summary: This architecture deploys an IoT Edge anomaly detection solution with a clear data flow: Edge devices run a custom AI module (stored in Container Registry) managed by IoT Hub. Telemetry and anomaly events flow from IoT Hub through Stream Analytics, which routes events to Cosmos DB for archival and to a Service Bus queue for real-time alerting. A Consumption Logic App triggers on Service Bus messages to send SMS alerts. The design favors serverless components (Cosmos DB Serverless, Consumption Logic App, Stream Analytics) to minimize operational overhead and cost for variable workloads.
overallReasoning.tradeoffs: Serverless Cosmos DB limits to single-region and 1 GB per logical partition — acceptable for most IoT anomaly workloads but requires migration to provisioned throughput for high-scale scenarios. Stream Analytics adds a small fixed cost per streaming unit. The AI anomaly detection module runs at the edge, so cloud-side ML infrastructure is not required — but the module container image must be built and pushed to ACR separately.

plan.validation: All resources deploy to eastus. IoT Hub S1 supports Edge deployments and message routing. Stream Analytics connects IoT Hub input to Cosmos DB and Service Bus outputs. Cosmos DB serverless is single-region (eastus) which is consistent. Service Bus Basic tier supports queue trigger for Logic App. No SKU or networking conflicts detected. Container Registry Basic is sufficient for storing a single Edge module image.
